scenes.text: Hello hello HELLO! I am Ms. Zara and today we answer the BIG question — WHO IS HEAVIER?! We already know about grams and kilograms. Today we go deeper — we ESTIMATE weights, ORDER many objects and become true WEIGHT DETECTIVES! Your superpower: knowing heavy from light without even touching a scale! Let us GO!

ESTIMATION — guessing weight WITHOUT a scale! Hold a mango in one hand and a cotton ball in the other. Without looking at any number, which hand feels heavier? The MANGO! Your hands are natural weight detectors! Good estimation means making a smart guess based on what you FEEL and KNOW. Scientists call this intuition!

ESTIMATION PRACTICE! Look at these objects — a feather, a pencil, a brick, an apple. Without measuring, ORDER them from LIGHTEST to HEAVIEST! Feather first — very very light! Then pencil — light but solid. Then apple — you can feel the weight! Then brick — HEAVY! LIGHTEST to HEAVIEST. That is smart estimation!

DIFFERENT KINDS OF SCALES! You already know the balance scale from the market and kirana shop. But there is another type — the WEIGHING MACHINE! It has a flat platform and a number display — digital or with a needle. You step ON it and the number tells you your weight directly! No balancing needed — just READ the number!

DOCTOR VISIT! When you go to the doctor, you step on a weighing machine. A healthy 8-year-old weighs around 20 to 25 kilograms! The doctor checks: are you growing well for your age? This is your BODY WEIGHT measured in kilograms. At home your bathroom scale works the same way — step on, read the number, done!

POSTAL WEIGHT! When you send a parcel, the post office weighs it. Letters are light — just a few grams! A small packet might be 200 grams. A big box could be 5 kilograms. The heavier the parcel, the MORE you pay to send it! Weight decides the PRICE. That is why your parents check before sending!

GRAMS for light, KILOGRAMS for heavy — but HOW do you CHOOSE? A good rule: if you can pick it up with just two fingers, use GRAMS. A biscuit (10 g), a stamp (1 g), a coin (5 g) — grams! If you need your whole hand or both hands to carry it, use KILOGRAMS. Your school bag, a bucket of water, a bag of rice — kilograms!
scenes.visual: Finger test for grams vs kg

scenes.text: ORDERING THREE objects! A coconut weighs 500 g. A packet of dal weighs 1 kg = 1000 g. A small lime weighs 50 g. Order LIGHTEST to HEAVIEST! Lime: 50 g — LIGHTEST! Coconut: 500 g — middle! Dal: 1000 g — HEAVIEST! Write it: 50 g < 500 g < 1000 g. Compare numbers to compare weights — they are the SAME skill!

ORDERING FOUR objects — expert level! Ravi weighs his things: maths book (300 g), water bottle (1000 g), eraser (20 g), lunch box (500 g). Order lightest to heaviest! Eraser (20 g) → Maths book (300 g) → Lunch box (500 g) → Water bottle (1000 g)! The water bottle is HEAVIEST — that is why your bag hurts your shoulders!

MATHS with weights! Ravi's school bag: maths book 300 g + science book 250 g + lunch box 500 g + water bottle 1000 g. Total = 300 + 250 + 500 + 1000 = 2050 grams = 2 kg and 50 grams! His back can take 3 kg. Is the bag too heavy? 2050 g < 3000 g — NO, it is fine! You just saved Ravi's back!

HEAVIER, LIGHTER or SAME — trick question! A 1 kg bag of rice vs a 1 kg bag of cotton. Which is heavier? They BOTH weigh 1 kg so they are the SAME WEIGHT! Even though cotton looks much bigger and fluffier, 1 kg is 1 kg! Weight is not about SIZE — it is about how heavy something actually IS. AMAZING and tricky!

Word problem time! Priya brings 4 bags to school. Bag A: 2 kg. Bag B: 500 g. Bag C: 1 kg 200 g. Bag D: 800 g. Which bag is HEAVIEST? Convert all to grams: A = 2000 g, B = 500 g, C = 1200 g, D = 800 g. Biggest number is 2000 — so Bag A is HEAVIEST! Converting to the same unit makes comparing easy!

Big recap — WHO IS HEAVIER? Estimate before measuring — use your hands and brain! Weighing machines give direct weight readings. Order objects using their weights just like ordering numbers. 1 kg of anything equals 1 kg of anything else — weight is not about size! Convert to same units before comparing. YOU got this!

WOOHOO — you are a WEIGHT DETECTIVE! You can estimate, measure, compare and order weights like a pro! Next time you carry a bag, pick up a fruit or step on a scale — you will KNOW exactly what is happening in maths terms. Ms. Zara is SO proud of you! Now quiz time — show your detective skills!

quiz.question: Without using a scale, Anu holds a stone in one hand and a feather in the other. The stone hand feels heavier. What is Anu doing?
quiz.explanation: Anu is ESTIMATING! Estimation means making a smart guess without using a measuring tool. When you pick up two objects and sense which is heavier — that is estimation. It is a real-world skill scientists and cooks use every day!

quiz.question: Order from LIGHTEST to HEAVIEST: 750 g, 1 kg, 200 g, 500 g
quiz.explanation: First convert 1 kg = 1000 g! Then order: 200 g is smallest, then 500 g, then 750 g, then 1000 g (1 kg). So lightest to heaviest: 200 g → 500 g → 750 g → 1 kg. Always convert to the same unit before comparing weights!

quiz.question: A 1 kg bag of rice and a 1 kg bag of cotton — which is heavier?
options: D. Cannot tell without a scale
quiz.explanation: They weigh EXACTLY THE SAME — both are 1 kg! Even though cotton is much fluffier and takes up more space, 1 kg is 1 kg regardless of what it is. Weight is about heaviness, not size. This is one of the most surprising facts in measurement!

quiz.question: At the doctor's clinic, the nurse asks you to stand on a flat machine that shows a number. What machine is this?
quiz.explanation: A WEIGHING MACHINE (also called a weighing scale) shows your body weight directly as a number — no balancing needed! You just stand on it and read the number. It is different from a balance scale which compares two sides. Doctors use it to check if you are growing healthily!

quiz.question: Meera has: book (400 g), tiffin (600 g), and pencil box (250 g). What is the TOTAL weight of all three items?
quiz.explanation: Add all three weights: 400 + 600 + 250 = 1250 grams! Book 400 + Tiffin 600 = 1000 g, then add pencil box 250 g = 1250 g total. That is 1 kg and 250 g! Adding weights works exactly like adding regular numbers. Excellent maths!
